corpname.part: Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections

did.abstract: The records document the activities of Artspace, a contemporary arts non-profit organization founded in 1984 and located in New Haven, Connecticut. The records primarily document Artspace's administrative history of fundraising, programming, and exhibitions including its annual open studios festival, its public art installations, and its annual benefit gala. Also included are materials related to Artspace's educations programs: Youthview, Summer Arts for Youth, and the Summer Apprenticeship Program. The records contain correspondence, meeting minutes, annual reports, and financial documents, as well as posters, photographs, and memorabilia.

bioghist.head: Organizational History
bioghist.p: In the late 1970s, a group of New Haven artists began to conceive of a space for visual and performing arts. This community need was affirmed in 1982 when New Haven Mayor Biagio DiLieto commissioned a task force on community art spaces, which recommended the development of an arts center to provide space for New Haven's artist community. Initially, an arts space was envisioned as part of the redevelopment of the Shubert Theater, but did not come to fruition due to budgetary reasons. Artists then conceived of an organization of their own, and this was the genesis of Artspace. In 1984, after receiving a grant from the National Endowment for the Arts, Artspace, Inc. was formally incorporated, receiving its non-profit designation in 1987. Early years of the organization were spent searching for permanent space at various locations in New Haven. Artspace operated from a storefront at the corner of Whitney and Audubon streets until 1985 when the Board of Trustees voted to become a tenant in a new building that the Arts Council of Greater New Haven and the New Haven Foundation were developing at the Audubon Arts Center. Artspace officially moved into the space in 1990 and spent several years at the Audubon location until 1998. Between 1998-2002, Artspace occupied 4 different empty storefronts until the City of New Haven and State of Connecticut helped it redevelop and operate from its current location in the historic Ninth Square, in what was a Civil War era furniture factory called the Chamberlain Building.

Artspace officially began holding events and programs in 1987. It presents a cycle of rotating exhibitions and performances each year, including an annual New Haven open studios festival which invites the public to view the work of local artists over several weekends. The largest convening of visual artists in Connecticut, the festival hosts hundreds of artists in spaces across New Haven, including the Marcel Breuer Pirelli Building, the Civil War Era Smoothie Building, and the Goffe Street Armory. Other signature programs include The Flatfile, a rotating collection of works of art on paper from local, national, and international artists. These works are for sale and are the basis for on and offsite exhibitions and academic study. Other sites for programming have included The Lot, an open space at 812 Chapel Street which presented temporary public art installations; and Untitled(space), an artist-run set of pop-up exhibitions in storefronts on College and Chapel Streets. Since 2010 Artspace has also hosted an Artist-in-Residence program.

In addition to supporting working artists, Artspace has offered several programs to foster arts education with local youth: Youthview, the teen docent program; Summer Arts for Youth (SAY), a mentoring program; and since 2001, an annual Summer Apprenticeship Program (SAP) that pairs New Haven Public high school students with artists who collaborate on the production and mounting of an artistic installation. The program was launched with Connecticut artist Sol LeWitt (1928-2007) and has been led by artists Titus Kaphar, Jean Shin, and Roberto Lugo, among others.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is organized into four series: I. Administrative and business records, 1982-2020; II. Exhibits and events, 1986-2020; III. Media, 1992-2016; and IV. Miscellaneous, 1986-2018.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.

Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository. The computer files are unprocessed and may contain sensitive information or be in a physical state that would prohibit use. Researchers wishing to request access should contact Arts Library Special Collections at haasalsc@yale.edu.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright is retained by Artspace for unpublished works they have authored or otherwise produced. After Artspace ceases to exist, or until January 1, 2091, whichever comes first, copyright passes to Yale University. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Artspace Records (ART 36), Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections, Yale Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The collection was originally arranged and described by the donor in 2016. The collection was originally organized into 16 series that generally reflected the order of materials as they were maintained by Artspace staff. The 2021 addition was integrated into the original organization before the entire collection was intellectually, but not physically, organized into 4 series to facilitate discovery and access. The previous description of the collection was only minimally edited. Also in 2021, transactional financial records were appraised and destroyed or returned to the donor. Processing staff have removed duplicate materials. Gaps in box and folder numbering reflect the separation of material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series contains administrative files, including planning documents, meeting agenda and minutes, and financial records. Materials also include early organizational history and locations.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in the series are generally organized chronologically by year.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series consists of the ephemera created for Artspace exhibition and business transactions pertaining to certain exhibitions, including mailers, flyers, catalogs, postcards, calendars, photographs, artist contracts, price lists, art checklists, and press releases. It also contains information about individual artists and their involvement in specific exhibits with documents and objects ranging from artist biographies, artist resumes, artist cover letters, artist proposals, and artist slides of artwork.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in the series are organized into: Artspace exhibitions, 1986-2020; Galas/Benefits, 1987-2020; and City-Wide Open Studios, 1998-2020; and generally arranged chronologically therein.

Garber, Abraham I. Gelbart, Earl Gordon, Barbara A. Harder, Lisa Hess, Edith Landowne, Greg A. Little, Michael Madore, Fethii Meghelli, Jane Miller, Sal Naclerio, Matt Pozorski, Beverly Rogers Judy Sirota Rosenthal, Cynthia Beth Rubin, Joe Saccio, Gerald Saladyga, Bob Santin, Sarah Schuster, Eva Scopino, Deborah Cooper Sidlin, David Sorvillo, Ruth Stone, Silvia Taccani, Ben Westbrock, Lawrence White, Joy Wulke, and Peter Ziou.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This contains one mailer advertising The Italian Artist exhibition, an exhibition of contemporary painting, photography, sculpture drawing, furniture, and decorative arts, curated by Linda Lindroth. The mailer also lists the names of all of the exhibiting artists (Mario Botta, Stephen Brigidi, Achille Castiglioni, Susan Farricielli, Mark Garofalo, Mario Giacomelli, Mark Gravino, Vincent Leto, Geanna Merola, Franc Palaia, Aldo Rossi, Joseph Saccio, Carlo Scarpa, Ettore Sottsass, Jr., and Silvia Taccani), donors (Giacinto G. Costa, Bank of America, Farricielli Oldsmobile-GMC Truck, Schiavone Corporation, A-1 Dodge Toyota, American National Bank, Richard A. Capobianco, Cesar Pelli and Associates, Perri Sausage, Inc., Mr. & Mrs. Philip Paolella, Raggs for Men & Women, Sloman & Diamantini CPA's, Soho – Odeon, Diglio's Restaurant, EST EST EST Pizza & Restaurant, Scoozzi's Trattoria & Wine Bar, DeRose's Marketplace, Inc., and Lucibello's Pastry Shop), the timeframe of the exhibit (October 8 – November 15, 1987), the opening reception details (October 8, 1987), the place of the exhibit (44 Whitney Avenue, New Haven, CT), and other contact information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This contains one mailer advertising Artspace's show of Connecticut women photographers, Photograph Women in Charge in conjunction with the International Women's Day and New Haven 350. The mailer features a photograph of Vivien Lu, by Inge Morath, one of the exhibiting artists. It also includes a list all the names of the exhibiting artists (Melanie Eve Barocas, Marianne Bernstein, Virginia Blaisdell, Dennis E. Bradbury, Marna G. Clarke, Jane Coffey, Peggy Ann Dolin, Joan Fitzimmons, Maureen Goss, Sandy Hale, Nancy Iddings, Karen Klugman, Lori Korowotny, Mara A. Lavitt, Linda Lindroth, Michelle Luke, Inge Morath, Barbel Scianghetti, Peggy Vick, Jane Booth Vollers, Karen Vournakis, and Gale Zucker), the shows sponsors (Milford Camera Shop, Photocommunications, Inc., Quick-Pics), the timeframe of the exhibit (March 4 – April 2, 1988), the opening reception details (March 4, 1988), the place of the exhibit (44 Whitney Avenue, New Haven, CT), and other contact information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This contains one mailer advertising Edgar Francheschi's show, The Song the Sirens Sing , featuring paintings, sculptures, and prints. The mailer features one of Francheschi's pieces called, The Return to the Venusburg . It also includes the shows sponsor (The Smart Family Foundation), the timeframe of the exhibit (December 9-30, 1988), the opening reception (December 9, 1988), date of the artist talk (December 19, 1988), the place of the exhibit (44 Whitney Avenue, New Haven, CT), and other contact information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This contains one mailer advertising Artspace's show in conjunction with Women In the Arts, Spirit in the Wood: Bessie Harvey's Sculptures , featuring artist Bessie Harvey, curated by Sal Scalora to celebrate black history and women in the arts. The advert features photograph of Harvey and a photograph of an Untitled sculpture made out of wood beads, and hair. The mailer also includes the advertisement for another artist Deborah Muirhead for the show, Spirit in the Paint: Deborah Muirhead Paintings . It also includes the list of grant funding institutions (The Connecticut Commission on the Arts and The New Haven Foundation), the timeframe of the exhibit (February 10 – March 18, 1989), the opening reception (February 10, 1989), the place of the exhibit (44 Whitney Avenue, New Haven, CT), and other contact information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This contains one mailer advertising Artspace's show in conjunction with Women In the Arts, Spirit in the Paint: Deborah Muirhead Paintings , featuring artist Deborah Muirhead, curated by Sal Scalora to celebrate black history and women in the arts. The advert features photograph of Muirhead and a photograph of her work, Tracing My Way Home (oil on canvas). The mailer also includes the advertisement for another artist Bessie Harvey for the show, Spirit in the Wood: Bessie Harvey's Sculptures . It also includes the list of grant funding institutions (The Connecticut Commission on the Arts and The New Haven Foundation), the timeframe of the exhibit (February 10 – March 18, 1989), the opening reception (February 10, 1989), the place of the exhibit (44 Whitney Avenue, New Haven, CT), and other contact information.

scopecontent.p: Consists of the ephemera created for each Artspace's galas and benefit events that feature an exhibition that consists mostly donated pieces of artwork for live and silent auctions or events consisting of activities that help support the organization financially. It includes the following materials: meeting notes, planning documents, business transactions, receipts, programs, catalogs, mailers, sponser information, autioneer information, event/gala tickets, bid sheets, and photographs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consists of the ephemera created for each of Artspace's annual City-Wide Open Studios (CWOS) exhibitions. Held over a number of weekends, local artists open up their studios to the community. In 2021 the festival was re-named Open Source. Series includes the following materials: business transactions, marketing materials, newspaper articles/advertisement, photographs, and official maps and guides.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series contains media materials pertaining to various parts of the organization, including unidentified events, exhibitions, and galas. This series contains document types that include the following: disks; CDs; DVDs; slides; VHS tapes; cassette tapes; negatives; and photographs.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in the series are organized by format type, and chronologically therein.
